coredata.dc:title: Ultrafine nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot structure and antibacterial activities against Bacillus subtilis 3610
coredata.dc:creator: Dwitya, Sat Septian
Hsueh, Yi-Huang
Wang, Steven S.-S.
Lin, Kuen-Song
coredata.dc:description: The potential of green synthesis of quantum dots (QDs) has been proficiently examined for future design and clinical applications. This research reported that GQDs and N-doped GQDs were synthesized and tested for their antibacterial activity toward Bacillus subtilis (B. subtilis) 3610. The GQDs were prepared by a pyrolysis method as nitrogen-free GQDs (N0) and considered as a green way to synthesize QDs. N-doped GQDs were synthesized at the different ratios of urea noted as N1, N2, N3, and N4-doped GQDs respectively via hydrothermal method. The particle size of the as-synthesized materials was found to change from 2 nm (N0, N1, and N2-doped GQDs) to 1.5 nm (N3 and N4-doped GQDs). The crystalline structures of N1 to N4 from XRD patterns reveal the carbogenic core. The optical properties of N-doped GQDs at 260 nm (CC) and 340 nm (CO) were observed in the UV/vis spectra. The amines and amides were confirmed for nitrogen-bonding functionalities of N-doped GQDs by FTIR and XPS measurements. The lowest concentration of the antibacterial of N-doped GQDs was spotted on the N2 and N3 to have inhibitory effects at 100 and 200 ppm. The minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) of the antibacterial of N-doped GQDs was spotted on the N2 and N3 to have inhibitory effects at 100 and 200 ppm and N4 was found to show the lowest bacterial cell growth. Moreover, this work demonstrates a cost-effective way of producing N-doped GQDs by hydrothermal method for antibacterial activity of surface chemistry (pyrroles).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Quantum dots
Hydrothermal method
N-doped GQDs
Antibacterial activity

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of GQDs and N-doped GQDs
Crystallinity and microstructures identification by XRD measurement
Particle morphology characterization by SEM micro-photos
Microstructure and average size confirmation using TEM images
Optoelectronic properties investigation by UV–vis spectroscopy

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Science and Technology, Taiwan

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We sincerely thank the Ministry of Science and Technology ( MOST ), Taiwan, R.O.C. ( MOST 105–3113−E−008−003 ) for financial support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of GQDs and N(1, 2, 3, and 4)-doped GQDs, respectively.
FE-SEM micrographs of (a) N0, (b) N1, (c) N2, (d) N3, (e) N4-doped GQDs (10 000× magnification), and (f) N4-doped GQD (30 000× magnification), respectively.
HR-TEM images and particle size distributions curves of (a) N0-doped GQDs, (b) N1-doped GQDs, (c) N2-doped GQDs, (d) N3-doped GQDs, and (e) N4-doped GQDs, respectively.
(a) UV–vis spectra and (b) FTIR spectra of N-doped GQDs, respectively.
XPS analyses for C1S of N(1, 2, 3, and 4)-doped GQDs (Figures (a), (c), (e), and (g)) (left side) and N1S of N(1, 2, 3, and 4)-doped GQDs (Figures (b), (d), (f), and (h)) (right side), respectively.

(a) XPS intensity of pyrrole-like N(N–C)3 N1, N2, N3, and N4, (b) Photoluminescence spectra of different N-doped GQDs, and (c) minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) toward B. subtilis after 24 h incubation.
Antibacterial quantum dots of methods and main findings.

row.entry: Un-doped CQD (C-CQD), N-doped CQD (N-CQD), and N-/S-doped CQDs
Visible-light-driven antimicrobial activity increases as the ratio of S decreases during N and S doping.
Inhibition of the formation of biofilm for the gram-negative bacteria and gram-positive bacteria.
The peroxidase-mediated perturbation of cell walls.
F- and Cl-doped CQDs are 0.151 and 0.284% respectively.
One-log10 reduction for Staphylococcus aureus and Escherichia coli.
Negative-charge CDs have the potential against multi-drug resistant Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The pyrrole-like group Intensities of N-doped GQDs.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Note: The intensity peak of the pyrrole-like group is at 400.5 eV.

head.ce:title: Ultrafine nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot structure and antibacterial activities against Bacillus subtilis 3610

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering and Materials Science, Yuan Ze University, Chung–Li District, Taoyuan City, 32003, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical Engineering and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering and Materials Science, Yuan Ze University, Chung-Li District, Taoyuan City, 32003, Taiwan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Seafood Science, National Kaohsiung University of Science and Technology, Kaohsiung, 81157, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Kaohsiung University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Seafood Science, National Kaohsiung University of Science and Technology, Kaohsiung, 81157, Taiwan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, National Taiwan University, Taipei, 10617, Taiwan
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering, National Taiwan University, Taipei, 10617, Taiwan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The potential of green synthesis of quantum dots (QDs) has been proficiently examined for future design and clinical applications. This research reported that GQDs and N-doped GQDs were synthesized and tested for their antibacterial activity toward Bacillus subtilis (B. subtilis) 3610. The GQDs were prepared by a pyrolysis method as nitrogen-free GQDs (N0) and considered as a green way to synthesize QDs. N-doped GQDs were synthesized at the different ratios of urea noted as N1, N2, N3, and N4-doped GQDs respectively via hydrothermal method. The particle size of the as-synthesized materials was found to change from 2 nm (N0, N1, and N2-doped GQDs) to 1.5 nm (N3 and N4-doped GQDs). The crystalline structures of N1 to N4 from XRD patterns reveal the carbogenic core. The optical properties of N-doped GQDs at 260 nm (CC) and 340 nm (CO) were observed in the UV/vis spectra. The amines and amides were confirmed for nitrogen-bonding functionalities of N-doped GQDs by FTIR and XPS measurements. The lowest concentration of the antibacterial of N-doped GQDs was spotted on the N2 and N3 to have inhibitory effects at 100 and 200 ppm. The minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) of the antibacterial of N-doped GQDs was spotted on the N2 and N3 to have inhibitory effects at 100 and 200 ppm and N4 was found to show the lowest bacterial cell growth. Moreover, this work demonstrates a cost-effective way of producing N-doped GQDs by hydrothermal method for antibacterial activity of surface chemistry (pyrroles).

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Micro-organisms, such as B. subtilis, pose an immense threat to human well-being. Antibiotic-resistant pathogens considerably impact the upsurge of bacterial contagions, which cause 23 000 fatalities annually [1]. Moreover, the high frequency of occurrence of multi-drug resistant bacterial infections and the lack of new antibiotics threaten the future of our health care system as we approach a post-antibiotic era [2]. However, nanoparticles (NPs) take part in an important role in addressing the issue as bacteria are less likely to develop resistance [3].

Recently, there has been a renewed interest in the antibacterial properties of nanomaterials, such as silver nanoparticles [4]. While the advantages of silver are generally perceived, little consideration has been paid to the expected dangers of the utilization of silver and its conceivable commitment to microbicide and bacterial resistance should be assessed. Hereafter, evidence has indicated that bacteria developed resistance to silver NPs and may be toxic to humans. Gram-negative bacteria developed resistance to silver nanoparticles upon repeated exposure to Gram-negative bacteria: Escherichia coli (E. coli) 013, Pseudomonas aeruginosa CCM 3955, and E. coli CCM 3954 [5]. The resistance originates from the production of the adhesive flagellum protein flagellin, which triggers the aggregation of the nanoparticles. Another study also supports that whole-population genome sequencing identified a cusS mutation associated with the heritable resistance that possibly increased silver ion efflux which develops the mechanism of resistance may be enhanced or mediated by flagellum-based motility [6]. When the size of metallic silver is shrunk to a nanometer scale, it can enter the body's cells and cause adverse health effects [7]. Considerably for biological concerns, Garcia-Calvo [8] found that CdSe/ZnS quantum dots severely affected the biological processes and neurological systems. These studies have suggested that cytotoxicity is a major concern regarding applications of metallic nanomaterials, such as quantum dots, in biology [9].

Although metallic NPs exhibit the above-mentioned drawbacks, understanding the behavior of QDs in depth allows us to explore their limits and develop suitable methods to regulate their toxicity for potential application as antibacterial. CQDs have received intensive attention because of their unique properties including low toxicity, high luminescence, robust chemical inertness, and easy preparation [10,11]. Furthermore, the CQDs have demonstrated antibacterial activity as a new class of antibacterial nanomaterials [12–14], and graphene derivatives are new potential materials for antibacterial applications owing to their unique characteristics, such as excellent solubility, biocompatibility, and relatively low cytotoxicity to mammalian cells [13,15]. As GQDs confirm a new antibacterial strategy, this approach is supported by the study performed by Sun and coworkers [16] demonstrating that the GQDs band-aids were prepared and exhibited excellent antibacterial properties with the assistance of H2O2 at a low dose in-vivo.

Very little is currently known about the antibacterial activity related to the surface charge of NPs. As gathered in Table 1 [17–21], a wide variety of methods of QDs for carbon structure has been developed to investigate the antibacterial activity with the specific findings of surface chemistry. Specifically, hetero-component doped CQDs with a molecule size of under 10 nm show advantages in antibacterial properties because of nontoxic properties, photostability, and surface functionality for very abundant and minimal expense applications [22]. As presented in Table 1, the negatively charged carbon dots (CDs), synthesized using a microwave, were studied against the Staphylococcus aureus [21]. In another study, the bactericidal activity was connected to the electrostatic interactions between the protonated structures of N-CQDs and revealed the great effectiveness of the N-CQDs [23]. These reports have supported the notion that GQDs of surface charge and nitrogen doping exhibit antibacterial activity.

Hydrothermal carbonization has gained enormous attention because it is considered an inexpensive and eco-friendly method for the synthesis of nitrogen-doped and functionalized carbonaceous materials at low temperatures [24]. Furthermore, carbonization in the hydrothermal method occurs under in-situ developed strain at low temperatures with little or no harmful by-product released into the surroundings thus, lying inexpensive, eco-friendly green chemistry ideas [25]. Given that citric acid can be easily and inexpensively obtained; it is a good source of carbon for GQDs-based samples and the device fabrication for the formation of the blue fluorescent citrazinic acid [26]. Moreover, the chemical compounds containing urea moieties in their structure have been shown to have a wide range of biological activities, such as anticancer, antimicrobial, antibacterial, antitubercular, antimalarial, and antiviral drug activities [27]. In the present study, with citric acid and urea used as the carbon and nitrogen sources, we fabricated nitrogen-free GQDs via a conventional pyrolysis-method, and nitrogen-doped GQDs (N-doped GQDs) using a hydrothermal method and evaluated their antibacterial activity against B. Subtilis. Time-dependent growth inhibition caused by the as-synthesized materials was examined by assessing MIC against B. Subtilis.

As shown in Table 1, carbon quantum dots synthesized in various ways have been shown to have antibacterial properties. Here, the hydrothermal method is feasible to yield quantum dot materials. Furthermore, the fabrication of N-doped GQDs with citric acid and urea sources is getting an interest in biological fields. Hence, we fabricated GQDs by pyrolysis and N-doped GQDs by the hydrothermal method and investigated the materials' fine structures. To the best of our knowledge, there is little understanding of the interaction between N-doped GQDs with microorganisms, especially on the specific surface by nitrogen doping of urea. Here, a green, inexpensive and green approach to synthesizing N-doped GQDs was established for potential uses such as wastewater of bacteria purification and biomedical treatments. Therefore, the objective of this study was to describe the N-doped GQDs’ surface chemistry of pyrroles on the bacterial growth of B. Subtilis. Furthermore, the crystallinity, particle size, shape, and surface function of the N-doped GQDs as the supporting information for the characterization of a specific configuration of nitrogen-doping was also investigated.

Analytical grade HOC(COOH)(CH2COOH)2 (99.5%) and NH2CONH2 (99.0–100.5%) were purchased from Kingmall Instruments Co., Ltd. and used without further purification. B. Subtilis 3610 wild-type strain 3610 (Gram-positive bacteria) used in this work was acquired from the cell bank of the Graduate School of Biotechnology and Bioengineering Yuan Ze.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of GQDs and N-doped GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: GQDs which were synthesized by the carbonization of citric acid as reported by Ref. [28] were designated as N0. The N-doped GQDs were synthesized by the hydrothermal method using the one-pot reaction in which 4.2 g of citric acid (as the carbon-graphene source) and urea (as the nitrogen doping) in varying quantities of 3.6; 4.2; 5; and 5.6 g were designated as N1, N2, N3, and N4, respectively. First, citric acid and the different ratios of urea were dissolved in 30 mL of deionized water. Then, the precursor solutions were transferred into a stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 9 h. Following the completion of the reaction in the autoclave, the solutions were first filtered through the filter papers with a pore size of 0.22 mm, washed with acetone, and then centrifuged to remove the supernatant. Subsequently, the N-doped GQDs were dialyzed (3500 Da MW) and carried out far from light to obtain powder for 4 h. The final products were then lyophilized.

B. subtilis culture was maintained at 37 °C in Mueller Hinton broth (MHB) or on 1.5% Bacto agar plates composed of extracted beef (2.0 g/L), casein hydrolysate (17.5 g/L), and starch (1.5 g/L). The growth assays were conducted in a minimal medium with overnight cultures diluted 100-fold and grown at 37 °C with shaking at 200 rpm for 12 h. The as-synthesized nanoparticles were added in an appropriate amount to achieve the final concentrations of 0, 10, 50, 100, 200, and 500 ppm.

Antibacterial activity is determined using the MIC method: the overnight cultures of 1 × 109 CFU/mL were diluted 100-fold using 50 mL of MHB in a 250 mL flask. The as-prepared materials were diluted appropriately to achieve the final concentrations of 0, 10, 50, 100, 200, and 500 ppm. The cultures were grown at 37 °C and 200 rpm for up to 30 h, and bacterial growth was determined by measuring the optical density at 600 nm (OD600). Initial cultures (1 × 109 CFU/mL) were prepared from the 50 mL MHB liquid cultures harvested during the exponential growth phase. Bacterial cells were treated with the as-prepared NPs at increasing concentrations (0–500 ppm) for 5 h, with continuous shaking at 200 rpm. Both treated and untreated cultures were serially diluted and plated in triplicate on MHB at the designated time points (overnight). The plates were incubated overnight at 37 °C and then subjected to colony counting.

This research employed several characterization techniques to understand the fine structure and the phenomena of antibacterial as-synthesized materials. The crystal structures of the as-synthesized materials were analyzed using X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns using monochromatic Cu Kα radiation (MAC Science, MXP18). The morphological and microstructural studies of the GQDs and N-doped GQDs were analyzed using field-emission scanning electron microscopy coupled with energy dispersive spectroscopy (FE–SEM/EDS) (Hitachi, S-4700 Type II). The particle size distribution and crystallinity of the nanoparticles were investigated using a transmission electron microscope (TEM) (Zeiss, model 10C) with an accelerating voltage of 150 kV and the average particle size of the as-prepared NPs was determined using ImageJ software (ImageJ is a Java-based image processing program developed at the National Institutes of Health and the Laboratory for Optical and Computational Instrumentation). Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (Varian, DIGILAB Scimitar Series FTS 2000) of the as-synthesized nanoparticles were recorded. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) spectra were recorded on a PerkinElmer FTIR spectrometer and a JASCO V-650 spectrophotometer. The chemical elemental composition of N-doped GQDs was analyzed using XPS (Physical Electronics, model ESCA PHI 1600) at an excitation energy of Al K α (1486.6 eV). The XPS signals of the Ni species were recorded using a CMA (cylindrical mirror analyzer). The two peaks appeared at ∼286.5 eV (C1s) and ∼400.1 eV (N1s) and were used as a calibration standard for C and N species. The XPS signals of the C and N species were recorded using a CMA (cylindrical mirror analyzer).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Crystallinity and microstructures identification by XRD measurement
ce:section.ce:para: The XRD is used to determine the crystallinity of the as-synthesized materials, as shown in Fig. 1 . The crystallization of N0 displays a wide band from 15° to 25° indicating the turbostratic carbon phase as well as the X-ray reflections of the graphene. The dispersed and weak diffraction peak of N0 located at the center peak at 18.5° is the probe of graphite showing a d spacing of 0.48 nm [29]. However, the XRD patterns of N-doped GQDS (N1, N2, N3, and N4) reveal sharp peaks at around 26,3°, which was consistent with the finding in a previous report examining the C-dots derived from citric acid and citric acid/urea confirming the crystalline nature of carbogenic core [30]. Additionally, on the N1 to N4, the domination peaks centered at 26.3° represents the hexagonal graphite plane (002) and may be related to N-doped GQDs surface defects or reduced oxygen by solvothermal treatment of urea and citric acid mixtures (180 °C). These XRD results indicated that the N-doped GQDs maintain a similar-crystalline structure to nitrogen-free GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Particle morphology characterization by SEM micro-photos
ce:section.ce:para: The particle morphology of the as-synthesized materials is depicted in Fig. 2 . As shown in Fig. 2(a–e), N0, N1, N2, N3, and N4 formed thin sheet-like structures with wrinkles and irregular shapes because of the stacked structure of graphene, at 10 000 × magnification. Hence, under 30 000 × magnification, N4 is found to be flake-like nanoplatelets, as shown in Fig. 2(f). These results are in agreement with those observed in the graphite particles with small and platelet-like crystalline forms of carbon [31].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microstructure and average size confirmation using TEM images
ce:section.ce:para: The microstructure and the average diameter of the as-synthesized materials are presented in Fig. 3 . The morphology of the N0–N4 (Fig. 3(a–e) on the left side) demonstrates uniformly distributed and spherical particles. Furthermore, Fig. 3 (on the right side) shows that the diameter average of N0 (Fig. 3(a)), N1 (Fig. 3(b)), and N2 (Fig. 3(c)) are ∼2 nm which are well consistent with those of N-free GQDs prepared electrochemically [32]. Additionally, the sum of nanoparticles is represented in Table S1 for average size distribution. The conventional and statistical method (Sturge's rule) [33] is applied to understand the particle size distribution. The average sizes of the as-synthesized nanoparticles are 2.6, 2.5, 2.5, 2.1 and 2.0 nm for N0, N1, N2, N3, and N4 correspondingly. Overall, the N-doped GQDs were successfully fabricated, and the morphology and structure of GQDs are unaffected by nitrogen doping through the hydrothermal method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optoelectronic properties investigation by UV–vis spectroscopy
ce:section.ce:para: The optical absorption of the N-doped GQDs (N1 to N4) is depicted in Fig. 4 (a). The N-doped GQDs emitted intense blue luminescence under irradiation by a 365 nm lamp (see inset). The emittance of N-GQDs blue luminescence is due to isolated sp2-hybridized clusters with ca. 3 nm within the carbon-oxygen matrix resulting in yields band gaps consistent with blue emission due to the localization of electron-hole pairs [32]. In addition to the size, the O-rich groups make an important contribution to the observed blue emission from N-doped GQDs. Typically, the transition peaks that emerged at 260 nm (CC) and 340 nm (CO) are π−π* due to the aromatic sp2 domains. The adsorption at 410 nm of N1 to N4 is a transition absorption of the surface molecular center. The long tail extending into the visible range is the absorption edge induced by n–π* transitions of nonbonding electrons adatoms [34] similar to our results. These results demonstrated that the as-prepared N-doped GQDs have remarkable optoelectronic properties because of N-doping-induced modulation of the chemical and electronic characteristics of the GQDs.

The functional groups of N-doped GQDs are investigated via FTIR spectroscopy as depicted in Fig. 4(b). The peak at 3450 cm−1 is the confirmation of the presence of oxygen functionalities (O–H and N–H stretching vibrations) as the spectrum of O–H/N–H stretching vibration confirms the successful surface passivation and doping of nitrogen [35]. The strong absorption peak at 1625 cm−1 is attributed to CO stretching bond referring to the amine bond [36] and primary and/or tertiary amides [23]. In addition, the successful surface passivation of nitrogen doping is the vibration peak at 1384 cm−1 (C–NH–C) [37]. The stretching vibration of N–H also validated the aromatic amines, primary amines, and amides [23]. Moreover, the presence of urea in hydrothermal reactions forms the doping of nitrogen of pyridinic and pyrrolic N-bonding configurations in the final carbonaceous composition [38]. As a result, the FTIR functional groups of the as-synthesized materials reveal the surface functionality of nitrogen doping: aromatic amines, primary amines, and amides. Therefore, the doping of urea in the N-doped GQDs from the FTIR result is confirmed and could be the factor that affects bacterial wall disruption.

As listed in Table S2, the percentage of elements of the nitrogen-atomic concentrations of N1 to N4 (increasing concentration of urea) were determined at 6.51, 5.89, 5.60, and 5.43%, respectively. Furthermore, the carbon contents of the as-synthesized materials are relatively high and correlate with the high substance of citric acid in N-doped GQDs. The N-doped GQDs of N1, N2, N3, and N4 were then characterized to explore their elemental composition and chemical bonds, and the two elements (C and N) are then distinguished into peaks at ∼286.5 eV (C1s) and ∼400.1 eV (N1s) respectively based on the full-scan XPS spectra (Fig. S1), which is similar to N-containing carbon-based materials [29]. Afterward, C1s configurations existing in the N-doped GQDs are de-convoluted with the Gaussian method at 284.8, 285.5, and 288.8 eV, and the three peaks are attributed to graphitic or aliphatic C–C-/CC; C–N; and CO respectively [32,39] (Fig. 5 (a), (c), (e), and (g)). The peak of C1s 285.5 eV in the N-doped GQDs indicated that the C–N bonding is generated during the synthesis reaction.

The XPS O1s spectra of (a-d) N1–N4 doped GQDs are shown in Fig. S2, respectively. On the other hand, the deconvolution of N1s configurations has indicated three contributions to C–N–C (399.7 eV) [30], pyrrole-like N(N–C)3 (400.5 eV) [40], and graphitic NN-H (401.4 eV) (Fig. 5(b), (d), (f), and (h)) respectively [39,41]. Similarly, the graphitic-N corresponded to the doping in the center and, pyrrolic-N and amine groups are particularly indicative of the nitrogen bonds on the surface [42]. The existence of pyrrolic-N suggests that nitrogen bonds with carbon in five member-ring systems can be doped [43] onto the as-synthesized materials. Furthermore, N-doped GQDs are likely sp2 aromatic and aromatic C–N with abundant nitrogen-containing functional groups species stemming from urea moieties during hydrothermal synthesis. The XPS result strengthens the existence of the pyrrole and amine group for the surface charge of the as-synthesized materials. The existing research regarding the nitrogen carbon dots confirmed that the atomic ratio percentages of N, C and O are unchangeable (Table S2) as the amount of urea added is higher than 3.6 g [40]. The result of the pyrrole-like group of N4 is the highest as shown in Fig. 6 (a). The full width at half maximum (FWHM) after deconvolution fitting is a useful indicator of chemical state changes and physical influences. The pyrrole-like group onto GQDs might play the inhibition as compared with N0 (free urea treatment), especially for the intensity. As shown in Table 2 , N4 exhibits the highest of characteristic peak of intensity indicating a change in the number of chemical bonds as it was treated with the highest urea precursor. Moreover, the surface chemistry roles of N(N–C)3 of N doping could be the major distinguishing factor of bacterial growth in B. subtilis 3610 as observed with the highest intensity. these results reveal that the graphene framework is shaped at this stage, which agrees well with the FTIR results.

The corresponding PL spectra of N-doped GQDs are shown in Fig. 6(b). The excitation wavelength of the PL spectra was recorded from an absorbance range of 300–400 nm and emission of 400–500 nm. It can be seen that N-doped GQDs indicate an asymmetric lump with the maximum PL emission recorded at 450 nm. The uniformity of the absorption band and PL band at 435 nm further confirms that the sp2 clusters contained in GQDs show high photoluminescence under UV light after doping and passivation with nitrogen [8]. In the reported one-step approach to preparing N-doped graphene quantum dots (GQDs) using pulsed laser ablation with diethylenetriamine (DETA) the exact N configuration that causes the enhanced PL in N-doped GQDs is unsettled [44]. Overall, citric acid is a better carbon source in the preparation of GQDs through hydrothermal routes with a stable temperature at 180 °C for 9 h consistent with the literature [43].

To study the growth inhibition of Gram-positive bacteria, the as-synthesized materials were examined by the MIC method as represented in Fig. 6(c). Our results revealed that there was no inhibition detected on N0 from 0 to 500 ppm. Further analysis revealed that N2 and N3 started to show inhibition at the lowest concentration of 100–200 ppm, indicating that the inhibition was at the lowest concentration of N-doped GQDs. Moreover, N4 showed about a 1-log reduction against B. subtilis 3610 at 200 ppm. No significant difference was observed between N4 at 200 ppm and 500 ppm. Furthermore, statistical analyses for our optical density data of MIC have demonstrated that the p-values of N4-GQDs at 200 and 500 ppm were determined to be 0.018 and 0.016, respectively (both <0.05), suggesting that the anti-bacterial activities of N4-GQDs at 200 and 500 ppm were statistically significant. However, the p-value between 200 and 500 ppm groups was insignificant (p-value = 0.274 > 0.05). This suggests that 200 ppm of N4-doped GQDs is the best antimicrobial dose compared to N0, N1, N2, and N3. The MIC result of the E. coli (Gram-negative bacteria) is also represented (Fig. S3). The complex structure of Gram-negative bacteria and the different thicknesses of cell walls are the differences in their cell wall structure, cell physiology, or degree of contact. The small channels in the outer membrane of E. coli have restricted nanoparticles to pass through to disrupt the inner organ [45]. The ability of N-doped GQDs to inhibit bacterial growth or kill bacteria was reported through complex mechanisms including reactive oxidative species (ROS) generation, breakdown of cell structure, fragmentation, and condensation of genomic DNA, resulting in a cytoplasmic leak [21]. Our result showed that N4 with the highest intensity of N(N–C)3 was capable of inhibiting bacterial growth with the highest among the other as-synthesized materials prepared. Furthermore, the cell wall of Gram-positive bacteria is mainly composed of peptidoglycan (d-glutamic acid) and teichoic acid (including wall teichoic acid and membrane teichoic acid). The nitrogen-containing CQDs were more likely to carry the positive charges, which penetrate the permeability of cell membranes that were relatively loose eventually the death of bacterial cells [46]. As the result, this finding showed that the nitrogen doping of GQDs (N4) with the roles of surface chemistry hinders the growth of bacteria via the breakage of cell structure by DNA fragmentation in B. subtilis 3610.

Our FTIR results show several characteristic peaks corresponding to urea and citric acid. Thus, it can be inferred to the inhibition of bacterial growth by the NPs of the as-synthesized materials. It is reported that surface functionalities of nitrogen-doped can inhibit bactericidal activity through electrostatic interaction [23] as confirmed in our FTIR analysis. The XPS findings along with the FTIR results also confirmed the dual role of nitrogen as a dopant and a functional surface group. One of the primary uses of organic compounds (e.g., urea) is the topical treatment of wounds due to their bactericidal and chemical properties [47]. In other words, the primary amino group of N-doped GQDs could easily interact with the bacterial surface through electrostatic interactions, triggering the complex mechanisms involving the disintegration of cell structure, and fragmentation and condensation of genomic DNA, thereby leading to the leakage of the cytoplasm [21]. The as-synthesized materials were observed with different intensities of the pyrrole-like group which is more likely the reason for the different growth of B. subtilis 3610. Similarly, the antibacterial activity against Gram-positive strains, in general, was higher than that against Gram-negative ones. This result can be explained by the fact that Gram-negative bacteria are more resistant than Gram-positive ones because of the complex structures of E. coli. It has been demonstrated that these activities of the pyrrole moiety result in the antibacterial activity of Gram-positive bacteria [48]. The N-doped GQDs with the surface chemistry of the pyrrole-like group could interact with the cell due to enzyme disturbance resulting in enzyme disruption.

Additionally, the smallest N-doped GQDs could interact with the cell membrane to gain proton as well as enzyme dysfunction. Moreover, the structure of Gram-positive bacteria consisted of peptidoglycan comprising of sugars and amino acids, the as-synthesized materials would target the osmotic lysis of glutamic acid. The intracellular bond was disrupted and cell walls were destroyed by the pyrrole-like group resulting in the mechanism of antibacterial activity of losing dysfunctional enzymes. As the result, this finding showed that the nitrogen doping of GQDs (N4) synthesized through the hydrothermal hinders the growth of bacteria via the breakage of cell structure by DNA fragmentation in B. subtilis 3610. It has been proposed that ammonia in the hydrothermal route functions as a chemical knife to polish the edges of CDs and results in the anchorage of N atoms on the surface of the CD [49] that urea which is a derivative of ammonia acts as a colloidal phase to form the CDs. As a correlation to the HR-TEM results, there exists a correlation between the antibacterial activity and particle size of GQDs. Additionally, the interaction of nanoparticles and bacteria cell walls was supported by the size of the as-synthesized materials. The TEM images have confirmed that N-doped GQDs permeated the structural integrity of the bacteria (cell wall). The smaller NPs can penetrate through the cell membrane of bacteria more efficiently, thus influencing bacterial growth [50]. Significantly, the agglomeration of the substances inside the cell even caused the leakage of intracellular substances, resulting in the death of the bacteria. However, the authors did not conduct the experiment on the ROS level in this study given that ROS is not generated in the inhibiting mechanism of bacterial growth [22]. Meanwhile, ROS activity could only be efficiently produced under light irradiation as an antibacterial agent [51]. The non-doping and doping nitrogen were explained by Kasouni [26] on the reduction of the number cells S. aureus cells by 70% after 4 h of incubation with 600 μg mL−1. It can be seen that antibacterial activity increases as the size of the CDs decrease [14]. Therefore, the observed antibacterial effect of N-doped GQDs towards B. subtilis (Gram-positive bacteria) 3610 was likely related to the electrostatic interaction(s) between the cell and the nanoparticles due to the nitrogen sources from urea, and the size of the nanoparticles penetration that causes to denaturation of DNA bacteria.

It is clear that NPs have exhibited bactericide activity and also appeared that the toxicity is not likely one single element for the bacterial killing. Similarly, proteomic and genomic examination of the impacts of NPs is further needed to be reviewed. For example, during DNA damage and presents as an Ag+ treated phenotype, the gene recA is expressed when downregulated whether Ag+ directly downregulates the gene to prevent DNA repair remains vague [52]. Hence, the environmental risk management to the spread of antimicrobial resistance is manageable given that bacteria can still evolve and develop resistance [53].

Based on our FTIR results, the presence of quaternary nitrogen doping in GQDs was identified and confirmed. In the research of quaternary ammonium CQDs, the quantitative proteomics-based tandem mass tags exhibited antibacterial activity. Most importantly, quaternary ammonium CQDs interfered with protein translation, posttranslational modification, and protein turnover in bacteria. Thus, the upregulating RNA degradation-related proteins were upregulated on ribosomes [46]. The biocompatibility of CQDs towards Gram-positive bacteria has also confirmed the empirical guarantee by proteomics and RT-qPCR [22]. Overall, this investigation would provide a feasible safe development for application) of carbon quantum dots in the biological field and environmental safety.

This work offers the study to examine the antimicrobial action of GQDs and N-doped GQDs, paving a way for these water-soluble N-doped GQDs as simple, cheap, and efficient candidates for broad applications. Metallic nanoparticles' toxicity and bacterial resistance are great challenges because of their excessive use. However, the electrostatic interactions and size of N-doped GQDs toward bacterial growth are beneficial and need developing further as a potential strategy to minimize the limitation of metallic NPs. The results demonstrated that the nitrogen doping to GQDs could significantly affect the bacteria activity of B. subtilis 3610 which is conditional on their chemical structure and more likely to the size dependency. Thus, the present study proposes a better combination of reduced-antibiotic ratios and N-doped GQDs composites. Furthermore, this work provided the role of the surface chemistry of the different urea on bacterial growth of B. subtilis 3610 based on the XPS results. The interaction of pyrrolic-N surface chemistry onto N-doped GQDs has played the role in the hindrance of bacterial growth (B. subtilis).

Sat Septian Dwitya: Investigation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Visualization. Yi-Huang Hsueh: Investigation, Conceptualization, Resources, Methodology, Visualization, Supervision, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Steven S.-S. Wang: Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – review & editing, Visualization. Kuen-Song Lin: Conceptualization, Investigation, Resources, Methodology, Visualization, Project administration, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing, Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We sincerely thank the Ministry of Science and Technology (MOST), Taiwan, R.O.C. (MOST 105–3113−E−008−003) for financial support.
